title: What is Mode Collapse?
concept: Mode collapse is when the generator discovers one (or a few) outputs that reliably fool the discriminator and just keeps producing variations of that same output, ignoring the true diversity of the training data.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the generator's goal during GAN training?

options.text: To produce outputs realistic enough to fool the discriminator into calling them real
To directly copy images from the training set pixel-for-pixel
To score real data as fake

exercises.explain: Right — the generator only cares about fooling the discriminator. Today: what happens when it finds a shortcut to do exactly that.

cards.title: The one-trick magician
cards.body: Picture a magician with twenty different tricks in their act. One night they notice the vanishing-coin trick gets the loudest gasp. So next show, they only do the vanishing-coin trick. And the show after that. Forever. A GAN's generator can fall into the exact same trap.

cards.title: What is mode collapse?
cards.body: Mode collapse is when the generator finds one (or a few) outputs that reliably fool the discriminator, then keeps producing near-identical variations of that same output — instead of generating the full diversity found in the real training data.

cards.title: A photocopier, not an artist
cards.body: A healthy generator should behave like an artist with a huge sketchbook, sampling something different every time. A collapsed generator behaves like a photocopier stuck on one page — technically it produces output after output, but it's really just printing the same thing again and again with cosmetic noise.

cards.title: Same face, different day
cards.body: Say a generator is supposed to create human faces. If it mode-collapses, you might ask for 50 new faces and get back 50 nearly-identical faces — same jawline, same expression, same lighting — with only tiny cosmetic differences like hair color, because that ONE face already fools the discriminator every time.

cards.title: "More training will fix it", right?
cards.body: You might assume that training longer lets the generator eventually explore other outputs. Often the opposite happens: the longer it trains on that one winning output, the more confident and convincing that single output becomes — digging the rut deeper instead of climbing out of it.

cards.title: Real diversity vs. collapsed output
cards.body: What you'd see if you sampled a large batch from each:

left.text:
Wide variety
Every category represented
No two samples alike

right.text:
Same output, repeated
Only 1-2 'winning' types
Barely any variation

options.text: A generator that produces only convincing cats, even though it was trained on cats, dogs, and birds
A generator that produces convincing cats, dogs, AND birds in roughly equal proportion
A discriminator that can no longer tell real from fake
A generator that produces low-quality, blurry images of everything

exercises.explain: Exactly — mode collapse means narrowing down to one (or a few) winning outputs and ignoring the rest of the real data's diversity.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Mode collapse means the generator produces low-quality or blurry outputs.
exercises.explain: False. Mode-collapsed outputs can look very convincing — the problem isn't quality, it's that the generator only ever produces one (or a few) types of output instead of the full variety.

exercises.sentence: Mode collapse happens when the generator finds one output that reliably ___ the discriminator, then just keeps repeating variations of it.
exercises.explain: The generator locks onto whatever output already fools the discriminator, so it has no pull toward trying anything new.

exercises.prompt: You generate 12 sample images from a GAN trained on cats, dogs, and birds. Which output is a clear sign of mode collapse?

options.text: All 12 images are dogs — same pose, same breed, barely distinguishable from each other
4 images are cats, 4 are dogs, 4 are birds, all varied
The 12 images are blurry and hard to make out

exercises.explain: 12-for-12 dogs that all look nearly identical is the telltale visual signature of mode collapse — narrow repetition, not variety.

exercises.prompt: You're training a GAN on a dataset of handwritten digits, 0 through 9. After training, you generate 100 samples and notice something odd about them. Based on what you just learned about mode collapse, what would that 'something odd' most likely look like?

options.text: The 100 samples are overwhelmingly one digit — say, mostly convincing '3's — instead of a healthy mix of all ten digits
The 100 samples are all completely random noise
The 100 samples perfectly reproduce the original training images pixel-for-pixel
The discriminator refuses to score any of the 100 samples

exercises.explain: Exactly! Next: let's walk through exactly this scenario — a GAN trained on 10 digit styles that mode-collapses onto just one.
